med_suggestions.exclusions: Severe allergy to antihistamines; significant sedation risk with other CNS depressants (clinician judgment).

med_suggestions.dosing: 25–50 mg PO q6h PRN itching (use caution)
med_suggestions.sig: 25–50 mg PO q6h PRN itching (use caution)
med_suggestions.exclusions: Avoid in older adults/anticholinergic sensitivity; glaucoma/urinary retention risk; sedation.

med_suggestions.dosing: 20 mg PO BID PRN (adjunct); adjust in renal impairment per clinician judgment
med_suggestions.sig: 20 mg PO BID PRN (adjunct); adjust in renal impairment per clinician judgment
med_suggestions.notes: Adjunct for hives in some cases; not a substitute for antihistamine.

note.soap:
S:
The patient is a 23-year-old adult presenting with hives all over the body. There is no associated fever, breathing difficulty, or fainting reported. No medication allergies are noted.

O:
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only history. No evidence of systemic symptoms such as fever or respiratory distress.

A:
 - Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute urticaria (hives)
 - Differential:
  1. Allergic reaction (idiopathic or triggered by allergen)
  2. Viral exanthem
  3. Drug-induced urticaria (no current medication allergies reported)
  4. Physical urticaria (e.g., pressure, cold)
 - Red flags considered:
   No signs of anaphylaxis (no breathing difficulty, no fainting), no fever or systemic illness.

P:
 - Disposition: Manage as outpatient with close follow-up if symptoms worsen or systemic signs develop.
 - Plan: Recommend oral antihistamines as first-line treatment for symptom relief. Avoid known triggers if identified. If symptoms persist beyond a few days or worsen, consider further evaluation.
  - Primary medication option (based on your answers): Cetirizine — 10 mg PO daily PRN itching/hives
   Medication protocol (from data/protocols/meds):
   - Medication recommendations (Tier 1):
   - Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
   - Cetirizine — 10 mg PO daily PRN itching/hives
     - Notes: Non-sedating option preferred.
     - Exclusions: Severe allergy to antihistamines; significant sedation risk with other CNS depressants (clinician judgment).
   - Loratadine — 10 mg PO daily PRN itching/hives
     - Notes: Non-sedating option; alternative to cetirizine.
     - Exclusions: Severe allergy to antihistamines (rare).
   - Diphenhydramine — 25–50 mg PO q6h PRN itching (use caution)
     - Notes: More sedating; avoid driving/operating machinery.
     - Exclusions: Avoid in older adults/anticholinergic sensitivity; glaucoma/urinary retention risk; sedation.
   - Famotidine (adjunct) — 20 mg PO BID PRN (adjunct); adjust in renal impairment per clinician judgment
     - Notes: Adjunct for hives in some cases; not a substitute for antihistamine.
     - Exclusions: Adjust in renal impairment; allergy.
   - Supportive care:
   - Avoid triggers: Stop any new suspected trigger (food/supplement/med) if safe to do so; avoid hot showers which can worsen itching.
   - Skin care: Cool compresses; fragrance-free moisturizer; avoid scratching.
   - Follow-up and safety:
   - Re-evaluation: If hives persist >24–48h, recur frequently, or you need frequent medication dosing—seek in-person evaluation.
   - Urgent/ED now: Any trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of lips/tongue/face, fainting, severe dizziness, or widespread blistering rash—call 911/go to ED.
 - Safety net: Advise patient to seek immediate care if they develop difficulty breathing, swelling of the face or throat, dizziness, or fever.
patient_instructions: Avoid triggers: Stop any new suspected trigger (food/supplement/med) if safe to do so; avoid hot showers which can worsen itching.
Skin care: Cool compresses; fragrance-free moisturizer; avoid scratching.
Re-evaluation: If hives persist >24–48h, recur frequently, or you need frequent medication dosing—seek in-person evaluation.
Urgent/ED now: Any trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of lips/tongue/face, fainting, severe dizziness, or widespread blistering rash—call 911/go to ED.
